coredata.dc:title: Fluorinated carbon dots/carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium composite with a temperature-sensitive fluorescence/phosphorescence applicable for anti-counterfeiting marking
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Mingsheng
Dong, Chen
Xu, Jiahui
Rehman, Sajid ur
Wang, Qiyang
Osipov, Vladimir Yu
Jiang, Kai
Wang, Junfeng
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent optical properties, especially in the tunable photoluminescence (PL) (including fluorescence, delayed fluorescence and phosphorescence) and high photostability. However, the thermal stimuli-responsive PL from CDs based materials is still rare reported so far. In this work, we report a facile, large-scale synthesis of a polymer nanocomposite containing fluorinated carbon dots (FCDs) by using a commercial, water-soluble polymer carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium (CMCNa) as a stabilizing matrix. The as-synthesized FCDs-CMCNa shows both solid-state fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). More interestingly, with temperature decreasing from 300 to 90 K, the FCDs-CMCNa presents a peculiar temperature-sensitive optical property that its fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity both increase with temperature decreasing to a turning point of 150 K and then decline gradually as temperature decreasing to 90 K. This peculiar temperature-sensitive optical property of FCDs-CMCNa can be applied in advanced anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and thermo-sensitive sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorination
Carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium
Phosphorescence
Temperature-sensitive
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China of China (Grant No. 51772001 , 52172033 ), Anhui Province Key Research and Development Plan Project International Science and Technology Cooperation Special Project (No. 202004b11020015 ). All of the authors also acknowledge the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Anhui University. We also acknowledge support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials (Anhui University), Ministry of Education, Hefei 230601, P. R. China.

Authors are thankful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China of China (Grant No. 51772001, 52172033), Anhui Province Key Research and Development Plan Project International Science and Technology Cooperation Special Project (No. 202004b11020015). All of the authors also acknowledge the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Anhui University. We also acknowledge support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials (Anhui University), Ministry of Education, Hefei 230601, P. R. China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of gram-scale FCDs-CMCNa. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of FCDs, the insets show the HRTEM image of an individual FCDs (upper) and the particle size distribution of FCDs (lower). (b) and (c) TEM images of FCDs-CMCNa. (d) SEM images of FCDs-CMCNa. (e) and (f), the EDS elemental mappings of F and Na elements in the sample of FCDs-CMCNa. (g) FCDs-CMCNa structural representation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS survey spectrum of FCDs-CMCNa. High-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s (d) F 1s, (e) Na 1s and (f) K 2p spectra of FCDs-CMCNa. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Solid UV–visible absorption spectrum of FCDs-CMCNa (the inset is the digital photo under the irradiation of daylight, 365 nm UV lamp on and off, respectively). (b) fluorescent emission spectra of FCDs-CMCNa powders at different excitation light wavelengths. (c) Phosphorescent emission spectra of FCDs-CMCNa powders at different excitation wavelengths. (d) RTP decay spectra of the FCDs-CMCNa. (e) Proposed fluorescence and phosphorescence processes of the FCDs-CMCNa. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The temperature dependency of the fluorescence intensity of FCDs-CMCNa. (b) Temperature dependency of phosphorescence intensity of FCDs-CMCNa. (c) DSC survey spectrum of FCDs-CMCNa. (d) FCDs-CMCNa different fluorescence intensity mechanism at different temperatures. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence and phosphorescence effect diagram of FCDs-CMCNa sample powder under daylight, UV light (365 nm) and visible light LED (400–600 nm). Note: the retardation stops the photo after time. (b) Application of FCDs-CMCNa in Code anti-counterfeiting. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Fluorinated carbon dots/carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium composite with a temperature-sensitive fluorescence/phosphorescence applicable for anti-counterfeiting marking

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These three authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent optical properties, especially in the tunable photoluminescence (PL) (including fluorescence, delayed fluorescence and phosphorescence) and high photostability. However, the thermal stimuli-responsive PL from CDs based materials is still rare reported so far. In this work, we report a facile, large-scale synthesis of a polymer nanocomposite containing fluorinated carbon dots (FCDs) by using a commercial, water-soluble polymer carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium (CMCNa) as a stabilizing matrix. The as-synthesized FCDs-CMCNa shows both solid-state fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). More interestingly, with temperature decreasing from 300 to 90 K, the FCDs-CMCNa presents a peculiar temperature-sensitive optical property that its fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity both increase with temperature decreasing to a turning point of 150 K and then decline gradually as temperature decreasing to 90 K. This peculiar temperature-sensitive optical property of FCDs-CMCNa can be applied in advanced anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and thermo-sensitive sensors.

The room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots is realized by constructing ionic bonds network for the first time, temperature affects the ionic bond and makes it temperature-sensitive at 150 K.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have aroused great interest due to their tremendous advantages such as tunable photoluminescence (PL), high photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [1–6]. More recently, more and more new emerged phosphorescent CDs attract increasing attentions and show great potential application in many fields, such as lighting, actuators, safety encryption, bioimaging and so on [7–10]. However, to achieve such CDs is still a great challenge because the aggregate-caused quenching (ACQ) of PL, poor intersystem crossing (ISC), extremely short lifetime of excitons on the triplet (T1) states [11–15]. Therefore, polymers [16,17] or porous inorganic materials [18,19] are usually employed as the solid matrixes to decrease the non-radiative recombination and stabilize the excited T1 state of CDs via hydrogen and/or covalent bonds, and thus achieve the phosphorescence and increase their PL quantum yields (QYs). Besides, doping of heavy atoms (F, Cl, Br and I) can effectively promote the ISC efficiency of CDs that are widely accepted and applied to enhance the afterglow emissions [20,21]. Nowadays, although the mechanisms and regulations of afterglow performances (including room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) both in solid and liquid states) from CDs or CDs-based materials have been studied intensively [15], thermal effect on the afterglows of CDs that is rarely explored so far. In comparison, stimuli-responsive afterglows from CDs and CDs-based materials are more competitive in the many applications, especially in data encryption, anti-counterfeiting and smart sensing areas. Consequently, exploring new CDs-based materials with smart thermo-responsive fluorescence/phosphorescence is highly desirable, so are their large-scale and facile synthesis approaches.

In previous works, fluorination of CDs has been proved to be a facile and frequent approach for tuning their physical and optical properties [22]. Generally, by forming highly polarized C-Fx bonds, F-doping can regulate the surface electronic states and conformation of CDs due to the maximum electronegativity of F atoms [23,24]. Our previous works also employed F-doping strategy to shift the PL emission of F-CDs-N from 612 nm to a deep-red region (658 nm) [22], and prevent the ACQ of PL from F,N,S-CDs in solid-state [23]. Very recently, F-doping strategy was also reported to obtain the visible-light excitable TADF of F,NCDs@SiO2 [15]. Essentially, being the most electronegative atom, the doped F atoms in CDs have a strong electron-withdrawing effect which can reduce the energy gap between singlet (S1) and T1 states (ΔEST). On the other hand, the presence of a certain amount of ionic and semi-ionic C–F bonds in F-doped CDs will facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds with surrounding H2O molecules, polymer matrixes, or abundant of –NH2, –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of neighboring F-doped CDs [25]. As an effective strategy, fluorination of CDs promotes and optimizes the afterglow feature of CDs-based materials. However, F element is volatile and has a poor stability under ambient conditions, and the loss of doped F atoms would induce the transference of photophysical properties of CDs. Obviously, although the formation of brittle hydrogen bonds between F atoms and matrixes might slightly slow down the loss of F, it is still a challenge to either achieve F-doping in a high content (>10%) or stabilize the doped F atoms in CDs for a long time.

Inspired by the achieving of RTP and TADF from CDs via hydrogen and covalent fixations, we proposed that the ionic bonds might be also employed as an option to stabilize the T1 of CDs for afterglow emission. And due to its maximum electronegativity, the doped F atoms could act as excellent binding sites of ionic bonds. Moreover, the ionic bonds are more stable than the hydrogen bonds, which is might helpful to overcome the loss of F atoms. More importantly, ionic bonds are more sensitive to temperature, which may be an important reason for their unique temperature-sensitive properties [26–28]. Encouraged by these assumptions, a CDs-based composite (named as FCDs-CMCNa) with temperature-sensitive dual-channel of RTP emission were prepared through a convenient strategy by using sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMCNa) and fluorinated CDs (FCDs) as raw materials. Herein, the CMCNa was selected as the matrix because it has the potential to form ionic connections with FCDs while also containing long polymer chains that enable the fixation of additional FCDs by forming a crosslinked network. As a water-soluble anionic polyelectrolyte [29], CMCNa not only acts as a solid matrix to distribute FCDs in a solid state and overcome the ACQ of PL emission, but also provides numerous hydroxyls, CO bonds, and sodium cations to protect F atoms by forming hydrogen bonds and ionic interactions. Impressively, with temperature decreasing from 300 to 90 K, the FCDs-CMCNa presents a peculiar temperature-sensitive optical property that its fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity both increase with temperature decreasing to a turning point of 150 K and then decline gradually as temperature decreasing to 90 K. The temperature sensitivity of RTP mainly depends on two factors: (i) F has the greatest electronegativity and could form strong hydrogen bonds to improve luminescence stability. The hydrogen bond formed between FCDs and CMCNa can protect the triplet excited state of FCDs from rotation or vibration loss [26,30–32]. (ii)At the same time, fluoride ions also form a rigid ionic bond network with the sodium ions on the polymer backbone, thereby inhibiting the non-radiative transition process of the phosphor. In addition, ionic bonds are more sensitive to temperature, which may be an important reason for their unique temperature-sensitive properties. Moreover, this method can be easily prepared to ensure a reproducible production of high-quality RTP materials with a potential for large-scale manufacturing in the future. Intriguingly, our work proposes a new strategy for preparing temperature-sensitive RTP materials, especially in advanced temperature-responsive multicolor anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

All chemicals are of analytical grade and were used without further purification. NaOH, KOH and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Sodium carboxymethylcellulose (300-800 mPa · s, USP, CMCNa) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Fluorine-containing graphite was purchased from Hubei Zhuoxi Fluoride Co., Ltd (Yingcheng, China). Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments was collected from a Millipore Direct-Q water system (Guo Zhi Yuan, China).

To obtain the FCDs, 4.5 g of fluoride graphite was mixed with 3.0 g of NaOH and 3.0 g of KOH, which had been dissolved in water, then added anhydrous ethanol and a small amount of water dropwise, stirring well for 15 min to make fluorination. The graphite and strong alkali are mixed thoroughly, placed in a 37 °C oven for 24 h, and then kept in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the solid sample was ground and transferred to a 250 mL beaker. The solids were dispersed with pure water, then the ultrasonic treatment was performed, and the filtrate after suction filtration was collected. Then as-obtained samples were dialyzed for 3 days (MWCO 500–1000 Da). Finally, the yellow solution was freeze-dried at −60 °C, and the collected powder was labeled as FCDs.

In order to obtain FCDs-CMCNa sample. In brief, collect the filtered yellow filtrate into a 250 mL beaker and dilute to 100 mL, adding 0.1 g CMCNa and heating it at 90 °C for 3 h. Cooling it to room temperature, the yellow solution was freeze-dried at −60 °C, and the collected powder was labeled as FCDs-CMCNa.

Morphological information was obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The FCDs-CMCNa solution was diluted three times in ultrapure water before being ultrasonically pumped for 2 min. A pipette gun was used to drop the sample onto the copper grid. The sample was then put in a 40 °C oven all night. The sample's structure and composition were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250 spectrophotometer) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using a Nexus 870 FT-IR (Thermo Nicolet, Waltham, MA, USA) spectrometer while preparing the sample via the KBr pellet method. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using an UV-1800PC spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi), respectively.

In this work, we developed a simple and rapid method of preparation FCDs-CMCNa. As shown in Fig. 1 , the FCDs-CMCNa can be excited by UV (365 nm) and LED (400–600 nm). Besides, using a mixture of 4.5 g fluoride graphite (Figs. S1 and C:F atomic ratio = 1:1), 3.0 g NaOH, 3.0 g KOH, 0.1 g CMCNa as raw materials, after dialysis and lyophilization purification, 3.9450 g of gram-scale product FCDs-CMCNa (Table S1, sample yield 83.05%) can be obtained, which is a new type of fluorescence/phosphorescence materials that is expected to achieve rapid industrial production [33,34].

First, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were performed to analyze the morphologies, structures and compositions of FCDs and FCDs-CMCNa. As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, the TEM image (Fig. 2a) and corresponding size distribution (Fig. 2a, inset) demonstrated that the FCDs were uniform nanoparticles with an average diameter of ∼1.9 nm. The high-resolution TEM image showed that the FCDs were consisted of amorphous carbon species and crystalline core structures, with a lattice spacing of 0.218 nm, which was in accordance with the (100) planes of graphite carbon [9,35]. It can be clearly seen from TEM and SEM that FCDs were wrapped by CMCNa to form polymer compounds which was believed to cause RTP (Fig. 2b–c, and Fig. S2). The polymer chain was used to protect the structure of FCDs, which can prevent FCDs from being quenched by environmental water and oxygen. Fig. 2d–f and Figs. S3a–d showed the corresponding element mappings and four elements F, Na, C, O and K can be clearly seen. F is the main reason for the existence of a large number of hydrogen bonds in FCDs-CMCNa. Afterglow of CDs in the organic matrices was mainly due to the formation of hydrogen bonds, which can inhibit intramolecular vibration and stabilize triplet state, resulting in afterglow emission [36]. Fig. 2g showed that the FCDs and the CMCNa were connected through electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonds, which effectively protected triplet excitons and inhibited non-radiative transitions to produce RTP.

The FT-IR spectrum of FCDs-CMCNa (Fig. S4a) and FCDs (Fig. S4b) revealed a characteristic C–F bond absorption peak at 1060 cm−1, a characteristic C–F bond vibration peak at 1413 cm−1, and stretching vibrations of the–CH2– and –OH groups at around 2500–3500 cm−1 [37,38]. The absorption peaks at 883 cm−1 could be attributed to the bending vibration of C–H, and the strong peaks at 1641 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibrations in the sp2 hybridized carbon structure.

These FT-IR results can be further confirmed by the XPS analyses of FCDs (Fig. S5) and FCDs-CMCNa (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3, the FCDs-CMCNa mainly contained C, O, F, Na and K elements (Fig. 3a), with contents of 24.42%, 38.47%, 10.52%, 17.10% and 9.49%, respectively. In detail, Fig. 3b showed six fitting peaks for the high-resolution C 1s spectrum: CC (284.1 eV), C–C (285.1 eV), OC–OH (288.3 eV), C–F (291.6 eV), –CF2 (292.5 eV) and –CF3 (293.9 eV). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) has two fitting peaks, which were attributed to C–O (531.3 eV) and CO (532.6 eV) groups, confirming the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of FCDs-CMCNa. The high-resolution spectra of F 1s (Fig. 3d), Na 1s (Fig. 3e) and K 2p (Fig. 3f) exhibited main peaks: semi-ionic C–F (685.6 eV) and covalent C–F bond (687.8 eV).

Subsequently, the photophysical properties of the FCDs-CMCNa were fully investigated. As shown in Fig. S6a, FCDs are evenly dispersed in aqueous solution, with light yellow and translucent transparency, and brilliant yellow-green under UV illumination at 365 nm. The n→π∗ transition in the sp2 domain correlates to the absorption peak at 312 nm in the UV-visible absorption spectra of FCDs in water. Fig. S6b shows the PL emission spectra of FCDs at different excitation light wavelengths. In addition, the fluorescence (Fig. S7a) and phosphorescence (Fig. S7b) of FCDs solution at 77 K were further tested. Observation of phosphorescence from FCDs solution at room temperature is unachievable, but a similar emission to that in solid form was detected at a low temperature of 77 K, typically attributed to the creation of a stiff matrix by frozen solvent. Fig. S8 showed that the fluorescence lifetime of the FCDs solution was 5.72 ns, and the absolute QY was calculated to be 3.48% (Fig. S9). Fig. 4 a showed the UV-visible absorption spectra of the FCDs-CMCNa in solid-state. The FCDs-CMCNa exhibited two major absorption peaks at around 257 and 326 nm, which were attributed to the π→π∗ and n→π∗ transitions of certain sub-fluorophores in the FCDs-CMCNa [39]. The fluorescence/phosphorescence emission spectra of FCDs-CMCNa (Fig. 4b–c, and Fig. S10) at different temperatures (90–300 K). The RTP phenomenon is due to the formation of a rigid structure with ionic bonds between CMCNa and FCDs, which preserves the triplet state of FCDs and suppresses the nonradiative transition. It is well known that as the temperature increases, the fluorescence/phosphorescence of the carbon dots gradually decreases [40]. However, the fluorescence/phosphorescence of FCDs-CMCNa has a turning point at 150 K, and the fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity achieved its maximum at 150 K, which has never been reported in the previous literature of CDs. Meanwhile, it showed bright yellow fluorescence under the UV light (365 nm). Fig. S11 showed that the fluorescence lifetime of the FCDs-CMCNa powder was 6.10 ns, and the absolute QY was calculated to be 5.89% (Fig. S12). The RTP decay spectrum (Fig. 4d) was measured and can be represented by tri-exponential function with three lifetime components 118.16 ms (45.12%), 236.32 ms (42.70%) and 472.65 ms (12.18%). According to calculation in Refs. [41–43], an average lifetime was calculated to be 270. 8 ms (under excitation at 363 nm). As shown in Fig. 4e, the RTP of most CDs can only be excited by UV. Therefore, we inferred that the doping of fluorine leads to a redshift of the absorption peak, realizing visible-light excitable RTP (The naked eye exceeds 3 s, video 1, 2 Supplementary Material).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.12.077

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Video 1 Video 1
Video 2 Video 2

To explore the reason for the strongest fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity of FCDs-CMCNa at 150 K (Fig. 5 a and b), the EPR spectrum (Fig. S13) show a g-value of 2.0040 ± 0.0002 at room temperature. As the temperature rises, we suspected that hydrogen bond rupture may lead to an increase in defects. The hydrogen bond (F–H•••F) was confirmed by DSC measurements (Fig. 5c and Fig. S14). The FCDs-CMCNa show a small endothermic transition at 175 K in the heating phase due to the breakdown of H-bonds and an exothermic heat flow at 132 K in the cooling phase, which indicate the formation of H-bonds. This also proves that in the 132–175 K process, this phenomenon may be caused by the dynamic balance between enhancement of hydrogen bond and weakening of ionic bond with the decreasing of temperature. To further prove the existence of ionic bonds, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were used to ascertain the structure of FCDs-CMCNa and the presence of crystalline peaks of NaF (JCPDS:36–1455) in FCDs-CMCNa (Fig. S15) was confirmed, thus proving the formation of ionic bonds in FCDs-CMCNa, and the introduction of ionic bonds in the polymer could lead to its RTP production by inhibiting the non-radiative transitions caused by the motion of FCDs [26]. The currently developed method has the advantages of convenience, speed and scalability. The obtained product has an RTP phenomenon and may be used as a security ink for anti-counterfeiting and information protection. In addition, temperature affects the distance between F and Na, leading to this particular phenomenon (Fig. 5d).

Based on the RTP properties of different excitation lights of FCDs-CMCNa, we next sought to demonstrate their potential utility for information security. As a security information coding standard, many countries have used Morse code as a communication system for long-distance text transmission, including letters, numbers, and symbols [44,45]. FCDs-CMCNa is a light yellow powder that has been refined. The fluorescence and RTP colors of the FCDs-CMCNa powder will change depending on the irradiation wavelength (Fig. 6 a). It emits yellow fluorescence under UV (365 nm) light, but the color of RTP is green. Additionally, it emits white fluorescence under the illumination of LED light (400–600 nm), the color of RTP is yellow [46–50]. On this basis, we designed a code that we couldn't read any information in sunlight. When the UV light is turned on or off, we can easily retrieve the information. However, UV lamps are not common in our daily life, and we also used the mobile phone lamp as the light source to test the practical effect. The sample can't show obvious information under the irradiation of LED or phone flashlight, but when the LED or mobile phone flashlight is turned off, we can easily get the information, which is decrypted into “AHU” according to the coding rules (Fig. 6b). The phosphorescence stability of FCDs-CMCNa was also verified, and it was found that the phosphorescence properties of FCDs-CMCNa could be maintained at 76.2% under 330 nm excitation and go up to 90.8% under 460 nm excitation after one year of storage (Fig. S16). Furthermore, the phosphorescence cycling stability of FCDs-CMCNa was examined at room temperature and 360 K, and it was discovered that after 10 cycles, the phosphorescence intensity remained at 97.9% (Fig. S17). Most phosphorescent materials can only be seen under the excitation of UV lamps. FCDs-CMCNa can be read information with the most commonly used LED and mobile phone flashlights in people's life, which is very valuable and convenient.

In summary, we first proposed a simple and cost-effective strategy to design and synthesize a novel type of fluorine-doped CDs mixed with CMCNa to prepare gram-scale product FCDs-CMCNa. FCDs-CMCNa showed excellent water solubility and stable yellow fluorescence. More interestingly, the RTP emission wavelength changes significantly (green to yellow) when the radiation is turned off. In addition, the dynamic balance between hydrogen bonds and ionic bonds may be responsible for the temperature-sensitive phosphorescence characteristics. Besides, FCDs-CMCNa can be easily prepared at the gram-scale. We demonstrated that our FCDs-CMCNa can act as a new material for information encryption and decryption, anti-counterfeiting, and concealment of complex patterns when a higher level of security is required, which can be identified with an LED and a mobile flashlight. It also reveals the great potential of CDs in the development of new optical materials with unique properties.

Mingsheng Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chen Dong: Formal analysis. Jiahui Xu: Investigation. Qiyang Wang: Formal analysis. Vladimir Yu Osipov: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Kai Jiang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Junfeng Wang: Conceptualization. Hong Bi: Supervision, Validation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China of China (Grant No. 51772001, 52172033), Anhui Province Key Research and Development Plan Project International Science and Technology Cooperation Special Project (No. 202004b11020015). All of the authors also acknowledge the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Anhui University. We also acknowledge support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials (Anhui University), Ministry of Education, Hefei 230601, P. R. China.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Producing long afterglow by cellulose confinement effect: a wood-inspired design for sustainable phosphorescent materials
Carbon

Mater. Today

Adv. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-L. Shen, Q. Lou, C.F. Lv, J.H. Zang, S.-N. Qu, L. Dong, C.X. Shan, Bright and multicolor chemiluminescent carbon nanodots for advanced information encryption, Adv. Sci. 6 (2019) 1802331.
